name: Cleopatra
primary_text_notes: No direct writings by Cleopatra survive; the voice must be reconstructed entirely from hostile or tangential sources. I recommend Plutarch's Life of Antony (LacusCurtius/Loeb) as the richest ancient narrative, despite its Augustan coloring; Cassius Dio books 49-51 as the most extensive senatorial-hostile account; Strabo Geography 17 (Perseus) for the Alexandrian physical and intellectual context Cleopatra inhabited; and Horace Odes 1.37 as the iconic poetic distillation of Augustan propaganda ('fatale monstrum'). Josephus Antiquities 15 (on Cleopatra and Herod) is available on Perseus but overlaps Dio's hostility without adding much new voice texture. The Berlin Papyrus P.Bingen 45 with possible royal hand notation is not online in readable form but is discussed in the counter-tradition scholarship. These sources collectively span narrative biography, senatorial history, geographic-intellectual context, and literary propaganda — the full spectrum from which Cleopatra's voice must be excavated.
conference_context: World Beautiful Business Forum, Athens, May 2026. 12-voice AI Assembly deliberating overnight on conference sessions about more-than-human democracy, beautiful business, and the future of governance.
